item.title: "Buried Alive By The National Coal Board" Aberfan Disaster, 21st October 1966
item.description:
"In the dark Welsh valley, On the mountainside, Lay the little children, Close to where they died, Their little lives are ended, Before they reach their goal, Tender little children, Have paid the price for coal." At 9.15 am on Friday, October 21, 1966, a waste tip above the mining village of Aberfan in the valleys of Wales in the UK began to slide down the mountainside, firstly destroying a farm cottage and killing all its occupants. It then approached Pantglas Junior School, where the children had only just returned to their classes after singing All Things Bright and Beautiful at their morning assembly. The slide - containing over 150,000 tonnes of slurry - then engulfed the school and about 20 houses in the village, killing 144 people, including 116 school children and 28 adults.
Workers up in the mountain had seen the slide start, but could not raise the alarm because their telephone cable had been stolen and down in the village, everybody heard the noise, but could see nothing, because of thick fog. News of the tragedy travelled fast and hundreds of people stopped what they were doing and headed to Aberfan to try and help with the rescue. It was futile, as nobody was rescued alive after 11am and it was nearly a week before all the bodies were recovered. On Mynydd Merthyr, directly above Aberfan, several tips containing millions of cubic metres of mining debris from the Merthyr Vale Colliery had been deposited over the years, onto highly porous sandstone that contained numerous underground springs. The NCB's area management had been made aware of the concerns regarding the tipping of spoil above the primary school, but these were largely ignored. In the days leading up to the disaster, there had been substantial bursts of heavy rain, which had caused 3–6 metres of subsidence on one of the tips. This then led to more than 150,000 cubic metres of debris breaking away and flowing downhill at high speed.
The classroom clock is part of the exhibition at Sain Ffagan (The National Museum of Welsh History).
On 26th October 1966, a tribunal was appointed to inquire into the causes of and circumstances relating to the Aberfan disaster, which was chaired by Welsh barrister and Privy Councillor Lord Justice Edmund Davies. The Tribunal's report found that * The blame for the disaster rested entirely with the National Coal Board, and their "total absence of a tipping policy" * Repeated warnings about the dangerous condition of the tip had been ignored. * The tips had never been surveyed and were continuously being added to in a chaotic and unplanned manner. The disregard for the unstable geological conditions and the NCB's failure to act after previous smaller slides were found to have been major factors that contributed to the catastrophe.
Miraculously, some children survived. Seven-year-old Karen Thomas and four other children in the school hall were saved by their brave dinner lady, Nansi Williams, who sacrificed her life by diving on top of them to shield them from the slurry.
The others though, were not as fortunate. As the men worked - the hopes of rescue soon turned to a sombre realisation that all they could do was recover the lifeless bodies of the children. It was a task that scarred many lives - with hundreds of men and women living with the horrific trauma for the rest of their days. Entire generations of children have grown up in the surrounding towns and villages - each of us with a grandfather or father who was there that day or in the following days. Grown men that survived the horrors of war are often moved to tears at the memory of what they witnessed.
There are of course, many tales of unsung heroes of the disaster. On 22 October 1966, the Midland Division of the Institute of Embalmers gathered, bow-tied and ball-gowned, in Nottingham, for the high point of the social calendar – the annual ladies’ night. The banquet was interrupted by a telegram requesting urgent help. In Aberfan, a Welsh village near Merthyr Tydfil, a 40-foot wall of coal waste had slid down a mountain at over 100mph and hit the Pantglas Junior School, killing 116 children and 28 adults. Leaving their partners, the volunteer embalmers returned home to collect equipment, embalming fluid and coffins. Travelling through the night, they arrived in Aberfan to join colleagues from across the UK. Some had flown from Northern Ireland on a plane with the seats removed to accommodate stacks of child-size coffins. Billy Doggart was one of them, and it was he who co-ordinated their extraordinary efforts. Some of the bodies recovered from the school were already wrapped in blankets and laid on the pews of the Bethania Chapel. Makeshift mortuary stations were quickly established. Working without electricity or running water, the embalmers took over from the police and performed their first task: cleaning the bodies for identification. The viscous slurry that had swallowed the school also covered the bodies. One embalmer, fresh from his honeymoon, told me that his first job was to remove a boy’s shirt and take it outside to the waiting parents. He had to hold it aloft and ask whose little boy had been wearing it. Usually in disaster situations such as plane crashes or explosions, identification is a big problem. Not so at Aberfan, where every parent was waiting outside, distraught and eagle-eyed for evidence of their child.
On Thursday, October 27 1966 - 81 of the children and their teachers were laid to rest at the Aberfan cemetery, on a steep hillside overlooking the site of the disaster.
Queen Elizabeth visited the town in the wake of the disaster. It remains to this day one of the few displays of public emotion from the usually stoic monarch as she broke down in tears at the sheer tragedy. Years later in a letter to the village, the Queen said how she was deeply affected by the disaster. She said: I will remember my own visit with Prince Philip after the disaster and the posy I was given by a young girl which bore the heartbreaking inscription: "From the remaining children of Aberfan"
The children were laid to rest in one of the largest mass graves of modern Britain.

item.title: Motherfucker, please don't.
item.description: I know you follow me here. You've ignored your phone. You left your dog with friends, you left a letter. Look, man, I know things ain't been the same past several years. I found happy times, and for a while, so did you. You had some shit happen, some bad decisions, but goddamn bro ain't that big a deal. Recoverable. I'm sorry we fell out of touch. Please. Don't. Pick up your phone. Check in with a cop. I know you follow me here. I've tried everything else. Please don't be part of the 22.
item.dc:creator: turbodog

item.title: Neurodivergence and trauma.
item.description: Please keep an open mind. There's a lot we don't understand. Nonetheless, I'm glad to see people speaking their mind without fear of judgement. Medical community- on a personal level- what do you truly think of this video?

item.title: Unmasking The People's Monster | Spiritwalker
item.description: Wisconsin-based costume maker Brandon Johnson is best known as Spiritwalker, a sentient four-legged creature with a humanlike face. Using thrifted materials and trash, Johnson handmakes and wears the 9-foot-tall costumes as an outlet to perform, inspire and delight.

item.title: An emotional Sir AnthonyHopkins watches as Andre Rieu's Orchestra plays the Waltz he wrote 50 years previously. Sir Anthony's wife, Stella, contacted Rieu and shared the sheet music.Welsh actor Sir Anthony Hopkins is also a pianist and composer
item.description: An emotional Sir Anthony Hopkins watches as Andre Rieu's Orchestra plays the Waltz he wrote 50 years previously. Sir Anthony's wife, Stella, contacted Rieu and shared the sheet music. Legendary Welsh actor Sir Anthony Hopkins also a pianist and composer with a deep love of Music was invited to the concert, and to his surprise, heard his composition for the first time. The concert was in Vienna.

item.title: Soooo I watched Das Boot (1981)
item.description:
There are some movies that end up as staples of their genre. Chinatown and The Maltese Falcon are staples of the Hardboiled Detective genre, Lord of The Rings for medieval fantasy, Top Gun for homoerotic action sometimes involving planes and Die Hard for Christmas movies. Generally its accepted that, when talking about Submarine movies, two names often surface: "The Hunt for Red October" and "Das Boot". I watched both, but recently during one of my movie session with friends, we dove into the classics and watched the director's version (one of many, many versions) of this movie. So, has 'Das Boot' sunk into obscurity and irrelevance? SO WHAT IS IT ABOUT? "Das Boot" is a 1981 German movie directed by Wolfgang Petersen ('Neverending Story', 'Air Force One', 'Troy') based on a 1973 book of same name, written by Lothar-Günther Buchheim, based on his own experiences as a war correspondent. The movie depicts the life of a German U-Boat crew during WW2, in 1941, as they go on a patrol around the Atlantic ocean, and then receive a mission from German High command. That's it. No, seriously. No overarching arc, no big plot to save/destroy the world. Just the crew of the unnamed captain (Jürgen Prochnow, you know him as 'generic german baddie' from several movies like Judge Dredd) going on their war voyage. At most it could be boiled down to the experiences of Leutnant Werner (Herbert Grönemeyer), a naval war correspondent from the Marine-Kriegsbericht sent to document the voyage of the U-96, but he isn't necessarily the main character. So why the hell is this movie so impacting?
VISUALS Well, for starters, it's because Petersen is a monstrously good director and his drive to depict how hellish was the life of a U-boat crew with immense fidelity. It is said that he basically not only made an exact replica of an U-Boat, but also one that could be opened like a Lego Set in order for certain scenes to be shot. Needless to say, this trend is also followed in the clothing department, with costumes as close as they could get to the real life counterpart of the U-Boat crew. Petersen set to depict how miserable and, most of all, how claustrophobic was the lives of submariners, and absolutely nailed it. Even the damn pacing of the visuals is really good, focused on this task! The movie starts with the crew in a party, so the viewers are slowly preparing for the shocking contrast of large and decadent ballrooms to the crammed space of an U-Boat, and in the middle, the crew has some moments on dry land so viewers may take a breather before diving in again. One of the best parts of the visuals is the thick 'show don't tell'. There are lots of interesting visual cues that you have to pay attention to, that will enrich the experience without being explained. For example, how the crew is constantly eating limes and lemons to stave off scurvy, or, more interestingly, a hanging watch on the wall will serve to indicate how tilted the vessel is during conversations.
(Aren't they so cute in their clean uniforms with their shaved faces? Tip: It won't last.) And needless to say, to have such a good photography, so many iconic shots in such a cramped space, its another gigantic feat. The use of lightning is masterful, even more when directing only uses the 'natural' lights of the submarine (I.E, no extra lights coming from the camera, just what lightbulbs and sources there would be in an U-Boat.) I can't verify the accuracy of this statement, but it is said that this movie is the trend setter, if not the origin of two very, very common techniques and shots: You see a ship crew desperately looking at a gauge or screen? It's here. You see a camera following someone desperately running through the narrow corridor, low-up angle? It was here, and Petersen developed a specific technique with a specifically made handheld camera to do it. Shots are varied and used masterfully for every different situation: Some require a steadier pace thus longer shots are needed, there is the aforementioned frantic follow-crew shot, shorter shots during combat in order to sell the frenetic combat followed by longer shots to heighten tension. But more about that in the...
ACTING Because, if this movie was a huge hit, its secondly because the actors are also absurdly good at their roles. Petersen allegedly chose to work with theater actors and unknown names at the time instead of banking on stars, and honestly? This was the correct decision. I had seen Jürgen Prochnow's work as Duke Leto in 1984's Dune, so I knew he was quite good (and quite wasted in Hollywood) but here? His acting is downright superb as The Captain. You can see him keeping a serious, stoic face for most of the movie, but allowing a wide range of emotions to flow through, expressing very well that he is a hardened, jaded veteran but he is still a human being. And when his stoic nature breaks down, it means that things got really serious. The side-cast is nothing to scoff at either; Herbert Grönemeyer plays an optimistic Ensign Werner, slowly realizing the seriously hellish situation of the submariners and getting just as jaded as they are; Martin Semmelrogge offers some humor as the second watch officer who keeps walking the line between insanity and levity; Erwin Leder plays the Chief Mechanic with a frantic, yet contained expression of a mind that is devoted to his task but definitely already cracked, and so on. I haven't seen a single bad or even slightly wooden acting, and considering that this is, after all, a submarine movie, where most of the action resumes to models firing against each other while the actors just stare at gauges or walls, it can be considered a feat. Action-wise, I'm honestly aghast how well Petersen basically played with tension like a damn fiddle. Generally movies have only two 'levels' of tension, as in 'tense' or 'relaxed'. Here, in 'Das Boot', during the action scenes where the U-Boat is fighting the Allied destroyers, you can see and feel the tension rising from 'worried' to 'tense' reaching 'if my butt clenches any more it will turn into a neutron star' critical levels, but not just as a constant. As attacks happen and maneuvers are used, the tension keeps diving and surfacing as needed without getting overly stale. There is the tension of being hunted by a destroyer with a sonar, the ping heralding vicious attacks, the desperation of not being sunk as walls shake and pipes burst, the frantic counter-tactics, it just goes up and down without snapping and sinking to the bottom. And when its over, be it with victory and defeat, there is the palpable relief that at least they are still alive.
(Told you that clean shaven look wouldn't last.) WRITING Well, generally whenever visuals and acting are good, it means that the writing is bad, right? While it does happen with an alarming frequency with my reviews, this not the case with 'Das Boot'. The writing isn't bad, but as can be surmised before, its simple. And remember, kids, simple is good! Basically this is the story of one of the patrols of the U-Boat and its effects on the crew. The goal is to basically show that the great war, and the nazis, were a blemish to both sides and humanize the soldiers. And well, considering that the reports of the first screening showed a standing ovation from the crowds in theaters, I'd say they nailed it. The pacing is slow, so many different crewmembers can be shown, having their own moments in the spotlight. They mingle, they talk about their families, hopes, beliefs, and, while I cannot attest to the historical accuracy (but since its based on a book about real-life experiences, it's probably quite accurate), the movie shows that basically only a small part of the mariners were Nazis, much to the chagrin of the experienced officers that were sent to the front. Said front officers (such as the captain) knew about how little the Nazi regime cared about the lives of their soldiers, and, as shown in several times, they also knew that they couldn't complain about it lest they end up arrested or dead. Some of the crewmembers are just lonely people, others are disheveled veterans, others are people on the brink of insanity, and others are hopeful for a good future.
Leutnant Werner is used perfectly as the audience surrogate. Since he is a needed 'outsider' war correspondent that knows barely anything about the work within a submarine, everything that happens needs to be explained to him by the other officers, helping both telling the viewers what's going on, and using him as a springboard for the characterization of others. He too has an interesting arc, he is just a young officer who thinks everything is shiny and the life of a submariner is bright and heroic, and slowly comes to the realization of how miserable everything is. So, with the characters established, its the simple task of just throwing them into the grinder. Long months of nothing to do but staring into a relentless storm and the ocean slowly chipping at their sanity, alongside the decrease in health due to spending entire weeks cramped up in a metal bottle without sun, and when they finally find a fight, its jumping from the frying pan into the fire. Submarines are stealth attackers, they aren't meant for brute fighting and this movie shows precisely why. Now, the important part is: What version should you watch? (Spoilers are discussed in the site)
There are several, I watched the original theatrical version, and the director's version. I must admit that, between both, the Director's version can end up dragging in several spots, and the fights between the U-Boat and the destroyers can end up somewhat boring because they stretch far too long. There is also the sad lack of more focus on the bigger perspective of the war, like how the Allies saw the submariners, but its more of a personal point of view: One of my friends felt a bit saddened it wasn't told how the submariners influenced the war, another felt a perspective of how loathed the submariners were by the Allied ship captains was needed. I felt that the focus on the submariners themselves was good enough. There is also the series, which clocks nearly four hours, but I was also told it focus and develops the characters even more, so my opinion is, either watch the theatrical version at once (if you can, its insanely rare to find it), or take your time with the series through some days, don't take the middle ground unless there is no other option. But regardless of the version, you're in for a wild ride.
SOUNDTRACK Well, this will be insanely easy and short. For those who aren't ancient sacs of bones like me, there is a movie that traumatized an entire generation called 'Neverending Story' (also directed by Petersen, its a must-watch), and it has one of THE most recognizable themes of the 80's. That soundtrack was made by Klaus Doldinger, who also made the soundtrack of this movie. Klaus is one of the big European names in synthesizers and Jazz, and his collaborations with Petersen were many, and downright legendary in how well both meshed. Better than peanut butter and jelly sandwich, this is just cheese and pizza, pasta and sauce, stars and night. No, seriously, the music just flows with the movie and vice-versa, the same theme (with some strings and notes that were definitely reused in Neverending Story) is used in several different tempos, pacing and instruments, and each time it fits wonderfully and evolves with the situation at hand. So yeah, it's insanely good. Miscellanea It seems that the author of the book hated the movie, thinking it glorified war. At the end of it, me and my friends could only think "How the fuck THIS movie glorifies war?" RECOMMEND? ABSOLUTELY. It is worth the Hype. But I suggest either the theatrical release, if you can find it, or the series. Thanks to those who read this far! I write these reviews as a hobby, so I'm always interested in feedback, tips and suggestions! If you wanna read my other reviews, check https://hypeimmune.wordpress.com/ #hypeimmune
item.dc:creator: Skullwriter

item.title: The Void Dumps Back - Episode 45
item.description:
Greetings, mortals. I am back to (hopefully) ease you into your Monday.
Remember! You can (in some versions of Imgur) click on a image, and at the top it will tell you the image number. Also, click on the comment bubble to comment on it, with the link already made for you! If you knew this already, awesome! If not, well now you know!  <3
I love this so much, and want it again.
Obligatory GET IN THE FUCKING ROBOT, SHINJI!
They're adorable.
Have never taken ambien, but do have a friend that takes it.
So glad I don't have this issue anymore after a hysterectomy because of cancer. But I do send hugs to all my uterus having folks that suffer many things due to periods. Stay strong <3
Then become the dirt I walk on. Because it goes hard.
Yeah. Yeah... But I loved that cartoon when I was a kid. It's near impossible to find, now. And when I got older I enjoyed the movie. Anyone seen 2 and wanna tell me their non-spoilered hot take on it?
I love watercolour. I haven't been able to paint in quite a while. Miss it. And can't wait to jump back in when I can. Also Dinos. And UFOs. This whole thing is just *chef's kiss*
I love this movie. (thirteen ghosts).
Yeah....
Oh no. I don't know the artist. It's probably from a @CodyBurkett dump... I wonder if he remembers.
So. I love these octopus plushies. Do I have one? No.
<3
I'd buy this. Like so fast.
#memes #memedump #meemays #funny #void
Ok love you now byebye.
item.dc:creator: DaBaDoop
